subject: reading
prompt:
The student wants to provide an example of a dinosaur fossil specimen's nickname. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Dinosaur fossil specimens can be found at science museums all over the world.
• Many dinosaur fossil specimens are given nicknames.
• The Great Plains Dinosaur Museum and Field Station in Malta, Montana, houses a dinosaur fossil specimen nicknamed Eleanor.
• Eleanor lived in the Late Cretaceous period, which ended more than 65 million years ago.
• It is a member of the genus *Brachylophosaurus*.
passage:
The student wants to provide an example of a dinosaur fossil specimen's nickname. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Dinosaur fossil specimens can be found at science museums all over the world.
• Many dinosaur fossil specimens are given nicknames.
• The Great Plains Dinosaur Museum and Field Station in Malta, Montana, houses a dinosaur fossil specimen nicknamed Eleanor.
• Eleanor lived in the Late Cretaceous period, which ended more than 65 million years ago.
• It is a member of the genus *Brachylophosaurus*.

choices.text: Dinosaur fossil specimens can be found at museums all over the world, and many of these specimens are given nicknames.
Nicknames are given to many dinosaur fossil specimens, including one housed at a museum in Malta, Montana.
Eleanor is the nickname of a *Brachylophosaurus* fossil specimen housed at the Great Plains Dinosaur Museum and Field Station in Malta, Montana.
A *Brachylophosaurus* fossil specimen from the Late Cretaceous period, which ended more than 65 million years ago, is housed at the Great Plains Dinosaur Museum and Field Station.

subject: reading
prompt:

While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Historian Isabel Wilkerson's book *The Warmth of Other Suns* is about the Great Migration.
• The Great Migration was a period in twentieth-century US history when over six million African Americans moved from the rural South to cities such as Los Angeles, California.
• To document this period, Wilkerson narrates the personal journeys of Clara Mae Benton and Arthur Coleman Ellis, among others.
• The book won the Hurston/Wright Legacy Award in 2011.
• It also won the Hillman Prize in 2011.
passage:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize how Wilkerson documents the Great Migration?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Historian Isabel Wilkerson's book *The Warmth of Other Suns* is about the Great Migration.
• The Great Migration was a period in twentieth-century US history when over six million African Americans moved from the rural South to cities such as Los Angeles, California.
• To document this period, Wilkerson narrates the personal journeys of Clara Mae Benton and Arthur Coleman Ellis, among others.
• The book won the Hurston/Wright Legacy Award in 2011.
• It also won the Hillman Prize in 2011.

choices.text: Isabel Wilkerson chronicles the complex history of the Great Migration in personal detail by narrating the journeys of individuals, such as Clara Mae Benton and Arthur Coleman Ellis.
The personal journey of Clara Mae Benton is featured in the award-winning book *The Warmth of Other Suns*.
In 2011, Isabel Wilkerson's book *The Warmth of Other Suns* won not only the Hurston/Wright Legacy Award but also the Hillman Prize.
Los Angeles, California, is one of the cities whose African American populations grew during the Great Migration.

choices.text: While winged kelp and arame contain umami flavor, umami can also be found in ham and mushrooms.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize the location of the figures in *The Space in Which We Travel*?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Marisa Vega is an African American painter.
• She is known for her large-scale, hyperrealistic paintings depicting African American figures in water.
• The painting *Between Us* (24 × 36 in) depicts a young woman with her arms outstretched floating in the bottom right corner of the canvas.
• *The Space in Which We Travel* (84 × 144 in) depicts two young girls in white dresses floating in the center of the canvas.
• She paints the water with vivid blue colors, including ultramarine and midnight blue. The mood in the paintings is placid.
passage:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize the location of the figures in *The Space in Which We Travel*?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Marisa Vega is an African American painter.
• She is known for her large-scale, hyperrealistic paintings depicting African American figures in water.
• The painting *Between Us* (24 × 36 in) depicts a young woman with her arms outstretched floating in the bottom right corner of the canvas.
• *The Space in Which We Travel* (84 × 144 in) depicts two young girls in white dresses floating in the center of the canvas.
• She paints the water with vivid blue colors, including ultramarine and midnight blue. The mood in the paintings is placid.

choices.text: At 84 by 144 inches, Vega's *The Space in Which We Travel* is even larger than the sizable 24-by-36-inch painting *Between Us*.
While the number of figures may differ, constant among Vega's hyperrealistic works is the placid mood that the paintings evoke.
In Vega's painting *The Space in Which We Travel*, two young girls are depicted in the center of the canvas.
Vega captures the water in paintings such as *Between Us* and *The Space in Which We Travel* in vivid hues of ultramarine and midnight blue.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 1 most likely respond to the claim about the potential benefits of Silva and colleagues' research in Text 2?
Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. It is also unclear whether categorizing music by genre is useful, since genre categories are ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic. As Mina Park and Daniel Choi argue in their study of the South Korean band BTS, relationships between pieces of music may be best understood with concepts other than genre.
Text 2
Gaúcha is a genre of music originally from Brazil that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the bachata genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Nadia Silva and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.
passage:
Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. It is also unclear whether categorizing music by genre is useful, since genre categories are ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic. As Mina Park and Daniel Choi argue in their study of the South Korean band BTS, relationships between pieces of music may be best understood with concepts other than genre.
Text 2
Gaúcha is a genre of music originally from Brazil that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the bachata genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Nadia Silva and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.
questionText: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 1 most likely respond to the claim about the potential benefits of Silva and colleagues' research in Text 2?

choices.text: By suggesting that future research may provide substantial advancements in the field of automated genre classification
By asserting that genre classifications may not be the most helpful way to think about music
By emphasizing that humans do not necessarily enjoy every recording in a genre they claim to prefer
By arguing that some genres are more easily recognized by classification systems than others

subject: reading
prompt:
Which quotation from *Life Among the Piutes* most effectively illustrates the claim?
*Life Among the Piutes* is an 1883 autobiographical narrative by Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins. In the work, Winnemucca creates suspense by emphasizing her physical response to an event, writing _____
passage: *Life Among the Piutes* is an 1883 autobiographical narrative by Sarah Winnemucca Hopkins. In the work, Winnemucca creates suspense by emphasizing her physical response to an event, writing _____
questionText: Which quotation from *Life Among the Piutes* most effectively illustrates the claim?

choices.text: "That same fall, after my grandfather came home, he told my father to take charge of his people and hold the tribe, as he was going back to California with as many of his people as he could get to go with him."

"Oh, how my heart jumped when I heard a noise close by. It was a horse running towards us. We had to lie down close to the ground. It came close to us and stopped. Oh, how my heart beat! I thought whoever it was would hear my heart beat."
"Oh, what a pretty dress my sister got! I did not get anything, because I hid all the time. I was hiding under some robes. No one knew where I was."

"Late in that fall, there came news that my grandfather was on his way home. Then my father took a great many of his men and went to meet his father, and there came back a runner, saying, that all our people must come together."

subject: reading
prompt:

Priya Raman and colleagues evaluated behavioral and genetic data from over 14,000 dogs, representing more than 100 breeds, and found that certain similarities in behavior between breeds correspond to genetic similarities between those breeds, suggesting a genetic basis for breed differences in behavior. This was the case for both energy and excitability but was especially pronounced for trainability, which can be seen when a dog demonstrates willingness to fetch objects. A different study found that the bulldog and the French bulldog breeds exhibit similar trainability behavior, suggesting that _____
passage: Priya Raman and colleagues evaluated behavioral and genetic data from over 14,000 dogs, representing more than 100 breeds, and found that certain similarities in behavior between breeds correspond to genetic similarities between those breeds, suggesting a genetic basis for breed differences in behavior. This was the case for both energy and excitability but was especially pronounced for trainability, which can be seen when a dog demonstrates willingness to fetch objects. A different study found that the bulldog and the French bulldog breeds exhibit similar trainability behavior, suggesting that _____
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: bulldogs and French bulldogs show a greater tendency toward trainability than most other dog breeds do.
individual bulldogs likely display higher levels of trainability than individual French bulldogs.
their similarities with respect to that behavior could result from a shared aspect of their genetics.
the two breeds will likely become less genetically similar over time.

subject: reading
prompt:
Which choice most logically completes the text?
Canada has classified the round goby as an invasive species that could harm some of the country's native species. But researchers Lucia Morales and Connor Hale have pointed out that "invasive" and "native" are labels that describe temporary circumstances. Changes in Earth's climate may force animals from their current ranges. Climate changes may also create good habitats in areas where a species couldn't live previously. In the case of Canada, these observations suggest that _____
passage: Canada has classified the round goby as an invasive species that could harm some of the country's native species. But researchers Lucia Morales and Connor Hale have pointed out that "invasive" and "native" are labels that describe temporary circumstances. Changes in Earth's climate may force animals from their current ranges. Climate changes may also create good habitats in areas where a species couldn't live previously. In the case of Canada, these observations suggest that _____
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: the country was previously home to some round gobies but they were outcompeted by invading species.
the country's designation of the round goby as invasive may be appropriate now but not in the future.
it's useful at present for the country to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the round goby.
even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the round goby will likely establish itself in the country.

subject: reading

passage: In May of 1987, Malaysia liberalized its stock market, meaning that it began allowing foreign individuals and businesses to invest money in Malaysian companies. This was part of a wave of stock market liberalizations around the world—Bangladesh in 1991, Kenya in 1995, and so on. The standard view among economists at the time was that liberalization would make it easier for companies to raise money from investors. Economist Thomas Avery examined the economies of 11 countries that were part of the liberalization wave and found that, on average, companies based in those countries received significant increases in investment in the three years following liberalization, suggesting that ______
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: companies in Bangladesh experienced a greater increase in investment following liberalization than did companies in Malaysia.
economists who held the standard view of liberalization failed to anticipate some serious negative effects of liberalization.
companies did not benefit from liberalization until at least three years after liberalization occurred.
empirical evidence was consistent with the scholarly consensus about the consequences of liberalization.

subject: reading
prompt:

Horses and sheep see in three dimensions (3D) by combining two images in their brains, one from each eye. This produces a sense of depth, helping the animals judge how close or far away an object is. Researchers have investigated 3D vision in praying mantises as well. In one study, Anika Ramanathan and her team fitted mantises' faces with two different color filters, one covering each eye, <u>much like the filters in 3D glasses once worn at movies.</u> By observing the mantises' reaction to projected images, the team confirmed that mantises do indeed have 3D vision, but it's unlike that of other animals.

subject: reading
prompt:
According to the text, what change coincided with Don Lorenzo becoming a patron of the theater?
The following text is adapted from Armando Palacio Valdés's short story "*The Love of Clotilde*," originally published in Spanish in 1884.
It was said that in [Don Lorenzo's] youth he once wrote a play which won him nothing but hisses and free entry for life behind the scenes of the theaters. Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Lorenzo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.

Whether resigned or not to the verdict of the public, he ceased to write plays and assumed instead the nobler rôle of patron to unrecognized authors and artists and to ruined managers.
Any youth from the provinces who arrived in Madrid with a drama in his pocket could take no surer road to seeing it produced than that which led to the home of Don Lorenzo. One and all, he received them with open arms, the good and the bad alike.

choices.text: He achieved public recognition for a short story he had written earlier.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among common minke and blue whales?
Like all species of baleen whales, the common minke whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the common minke whale small—it can have a mass as high as 4,000 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the blue whale, which can weigh a whopping 150,000 kg and consume as much as 45,000 kg of krill per day.
passage: Like all species of baleen whales, the common minke whale feeds on tiny creatures known as krill by filtering water through bristle-like keratin structures called baleen plates. In this way, baleen whales can eat up to 30 percent of their total mass per day. And while no one would call the common minke whale small—it can have a mass as high as 4,000 kg—it is one of the smaller baleen whales and is much smaller than the blue whale, which can weigh a whopping 150,000 kg and consume as much as 45,000 kg of krill per day.
questionText: Based on the text, what can most reasonably be concluded about krill consumption among common minke and blue whales?

choices.text: Most baleen whales include krill in their diets, but the common minke whale is less likely than the blue whale to do so.
Both the common minke whale and the blue whale can eat as much as 45,000 kg of krill per day.
The blue whale is able to eat more krill per day than the common minke whale is.
The quantities of krill consumed by blue whales has made it difficult for common minke whales to find sufficient food.

But while no source before 1155 includes references to the famous Round Table at which Arthur's knights assembled, both the *Roman de Brut* and Sir Thomas Malory's 15th-century compilation of Arthurian legends, *Le Morte d'Arthur*, do. But while no source before 1155 includes references to the famous Round Table at which Arthur's knights assembled, both the *Roman de Brut* and Sir Thomas Malory's 15th-century compilation of Arthurian legends, *Le Morte d'Arthur*, do. It can therefore be inferred that ___
questionText: Which choice most logically completes the text?

choices.text: Geoffrey of Monmouth was unaware of stories of the Round Table when composing his *History*, though historians know that works containing such stories were available to him.

choices.text: participants regarded the experiences of both the doctors and former trial volunteers as relevant to the subject of clinical trials but were skeptical of the doctors' status as credentialed experts.

the fact that former trial volunteers went through the same experience that participants were contemplating while doctors did not was more important to participants than the doctors' status as credentialed experts was.

• The painting *Yesterday Called and Said We Were Together* (48 × 60 in) depicts two unidentifiable figures with their arms outstretched floating in the upper left of the canvas.

passage:
Which choice most effectively uses information from the given sentences to emphasize the location of the figure in *New Day Coming*?
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
• Calida Garcia Rawles is an African American painter.
• She is known for her large-scale, hyperrealistic paintings depicting African American figures in water.
• The painting *Yesterday Called and Said We Were Together* (48 × 60 in) depicts two unidentifiable figures with their arms outstretched floating in the upper left of the canvas.
• *New Day Coming* (48 × 120 in) depicts a woman in a white dress floating on the left side of the canvas.
• She paints the water with vivid blue colors, including mint blue and navy.
• The mood in the paintings is tranquil.

choices.text: While the number of figures may differ, constant among Rawles's hyperrealistic works is the tranquil mood that the paintings evoke.
In Rawles's painting *New Day Coming*, a woman is depicted on the left side of the canvas.
Rawles captures the water in paintings such as *Yesterday Called and Said We Were Together* and *New Day Coming* in vivid hues of mint blue and navy.
At 48 by 120 inches, Rawles's *New Day Coming* is even larger than the sizable 48-by-60-inch painting *Yesterday Called and Said We Were Together*.

subject: reading
prompt:
Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 1 most likely respond to the claim about the potential benefits of Silva and colleagues' research in Text 2?
Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. It is also unclear whether categorizing music by genre is useful, since genre categories are ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic. As Mina Park and Daniel Kim argue in their study of the South Korean band BTS, relationships between pieces of music may be best understood with concepts other than genre.
Text 2
Tango is a genre of music originally from Argentina and Uruguay that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the axé genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Mateo Silva and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.
passage:
Text 1
Attempts to automate classification of music into genres have not been very successful. It is also unclear whether categorizing music by genre is useful, since genre categories are ambiguous, subjective, and simplistic. As Mina Park and Daniel Kim argue in their study of the South Korean band BTS, relationships between pieces of music may be best understood with concepts other than genre.
Text 2
Tango is a genre of music originally from Argentina and Uruguay that shares some harmonic and rhythmic similarities with the axé genre. Automated genre classification systems typically struggle to draw distinctions in situations like this, but Mateo Silva and colleagues solved that problem by converting sound to images and having computers compare features of those images. Their approach could improve genre classification, which could have many benefits for users.
questionText: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 1 most likely respond to the claim about the potential benefits of Silva and colleagues' research in Text 2?

choices.text: By emphasizing that humans do not necessarily enjoy every recording in a genre they claim to prefer
By asserting that genre classifications may not be the most helpful way to think about music
By arguing that some genres are more easily recognized by classification systems than others
By suggesting that future research may provide substantial advancements in the field of automated genre classification
